section.title: Othniel
section.sectionRationale: This is the first full turn of the cycle that will shape the whole book, and it is presented cleanly before every later judge complicates it. Israel does evil, the LORD sells them into the hand of Cushan-rishathaim, they cry out, the Spirit of the LORD raises up Othniel, he delivers, and the land rests forty years. Othniel is the model judge, and his brief account is the pattern by which all the others will be measured and found to decay.
section.thesis: When Israel forgets the LORD and is handed over to an oppressor, their cry rises and the LORD Himself raises up and empowers a deliverer by His Spirit, so that salvation is shown from the outset to be God's own work of grace for a people who deserve only judgment.
section.frameNote: Five beats of a single cycle: Israel's evil, verse 7; God's selling them into servitude, verse 8; the cry and the raised-up deliverer, verse 9; the Spirit and the victory, verse 10; and the land's rest, verse 11.

units.label: Israel forgets the LORD
units.note: they did evil and served the Baals

verses.text: So the Israelites did evil in the sight of the LORD; they forgot the LORD their God and served the Baals and the Asherahs.

units.label: Sold into servitude
units.note: the anger of the LORD burned

verses.text: Then the anger of the LORD burned against Israel, and He sold them into the hand of Cushan-rishathaim king of Aram-naharaim, and the Israelites served him eight years.

units.label: The cry and the deliverer
units.note: the LORD raised up a saviour

verses.text: But when the Israelites cried out to the LORD, He raised up Othniel son of Caleb’s younger brother Kenaz as a {{moshia|deliverer}} to {{yasha|save}} them.

units.label: The Spirit and the victory
units.note: the Spirit of the LORD came upon him

verses.text: The {{ruach|Spirit}} of the LORD came upon him, and he became Israel’s {{shophet|judge}} and went out to war. And the LORD delivered Cushan-rishathaim king of Aram into the hand of Othniel, who prevailed against him.
So the land had rest for forty years, until Othniel son of Kenaz died.

moshia.gloss: A deliverer or saviour, one who rescues. It is a participle from the verb to save, yasha, and the same word is used of the LORD Himself as Israel's Saviour, Isaiah 43:11. Here it names Othniel, but the raising up of the saviour is God's own act, so that even the human deliverer points beyond himself to the God who saves.

yasha.gloss: To save, deliver, rescue, bring victory. This is the drumbeat verb of the whole book: Israel cannot save itself, so the LORD raises up a saviour to save them. The name Jesus, Yeshua, comes from this same root and means the LORD saves, Matthew 1:21, so every deliverance in Judges is a small foreshadowing of the great salvation to come.

ruach.gloss: Spirit, breath, wind. The Spirit of the LORD coming upon a judge is what turns an ordinary Israelite into God's instrument of rescue; the power is not native to Othniel but given. The same Spirit rests without measure on Christ, Isaiah 11:2 and Luke 4:18, the true Judge and Deliverer.

shophet.gloss: To judge, govern, deliver, set right. A shophet, judge, in this book is not mainly a courtroom official but a Spirit-raised rescuer and leader who sets God's people right by defeating their oppressors. The word gives the book its name and looks ahead to the righteous King and Judge who will one day set all things right.

basic.context: This short account is the first complete turn of the cycle that will govern the entire book of Judges. Israel forgets the LORD and serves the Baals and the Asherahs, the gods of the surrounding nations. In just anger the LORD sells them into the hand of Cushan-rishathaim, a king from Mesopotamia, and they serve him eight years. When they cry out, the LORD raises up Othniel, Caleb's younger relative and a man already introduced as a faithful warrior in chapter one, as their deliverer. The Spirit of the LORD comes on him, he judges Israel and goes to war, and God hands the enemy over to him. The land then rests forty years. The key question is: who actually saves Israel? The passage answers plainly, the LORD does, through a Spirit-empowered deliverer.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative that also functions as a template. Othniel is deliberately presented as the model judge, with no flaws recorded, so that the pattern stands clean before the later judges begin to distort it. The account describes what happened; it does not command us to seek military deliverers today. What endures is the shape of grace beneath the story: sin brings God's just discipline, the cry of the helpless is heard, and salvation comes down from God, not up from human strength.
basic.originalLanguages: The passage turns on a cluster of words. Israel needs a moshia, a deliverer or saviour, and the LORD raises one up to yasha, to save, them. The verb yasha is the heartbeat of the book, and the name Jesus comes from that very root. Othniel becomes Israel's shophet, its judge, not a courtroom official but a Spirit-raised rescuer, once the ruach, the Spirit of the LORD, comes upon him. The power is given, not native.
basic.typology: Othniel is a small, early type of Christ: a deliverer raised up by God, empowered by the Spirit, who saves a helpless people and secures their rest. His forty years of rest foreshadow the true and lasting rest that Christ gives, Hebrews 4. Every judge who saves Israel is a shadow; the substance is Jesus, the LORD who saves.
basic.christ: The LORD raises up a saviour on whom the Spirit rests, who defeats the enemy and wins rest for God's people. This points straight to Jesus, the true Deliverer on whom the Spirit descended without measure, who conquers the greater enemies of sin, death, and the devil, and who gives His people a rest that never ends. Where Othniel's rest lasted forty years and then he died, Christ lives forever and His salvation never lapses.
basic.god: God is holy and just: He will not overlook His people's idolatry, and His anger against sin is real. Yet He is patient and merciful: the moment they cry out, He acts to save. Crucially, He is the true author of the rescue. He raises up the deliverer, He sends the Spirit, and He hands the enemy over. Salvation is His work of grace from beginning to end.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 28 warned that covenant unfaithfulness would bring servitude to enemies, exactly what happens here. Joshua 15 and Judges 1 introduce Othniel as a proven warrior who won Caleb's daughter. Isaiah 43 declares that the LORD alone is Israel's Saviour. And Matthew 1 names Jesus because He will save His people from their sins.
basic.oneStory: Israel, freshly settled in the land, forgets the God who saved them and falls into slavery, and God graciously raises up a saviour to bring them back to rest. This first clean turn of the cycle sets the pattern for the whole book and, beyond it, for the whole Bible: a people who cannot save themselves, and a God who keeps raising up deliverers until He sends the final one, Jesus, the LORD who saves.

context.literary: This is the paradigm account, the pattern-setting cycle that the narrator lays down cleanly before the later judges complicate and darken it. Every element that will recur is here in compact form: evil, anger, servitude, cry, a raised-up deliverer, the Spirit, victory, and rest, followed by the death of the judge. Othniel is given no personal flaws and almost no personal detail, which is the point: he is the standard against which Ehud, Gideon, Jephthah, and Samson will each fall short.
context.historical: The judges period follows the conquest and settlement under Joshua, roughly the centuries before Israel's monarchy. Cushan-rishathaim, king of Aram-naharaim, comes from Mesopotamia, the region between the rivers in the northeast; his name may carry a mocking sense of doubled wickedness. Othniel is from the tribe of Judah, connected to Caleb, and had already distinguished himself capturing Kiriath-sepher, Judges 1:11-13.
context.canonical: Judges sits between Joshua's conquest and the rise of the monarchy, tracing Israel's downward spiral when everyone did what was right in his own eyes. Othniel's account establishes the salvation pattern, sin, cry, deliverer, rest, that recurs throughout Scripture and finds its fulfillment in Christ. The refrain that the land had rest joins the Bible's long theme of the rest God gives, from Canaan to the Sabbath rest that remains for the people of God, Hebrews 4:9.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To present the cycle of Israel's apostasy and God's saving grace in its purest, model form through Othniel, so that readers see clearly both the justice of God against idolatry and the mercy of God who raises up a Spirit-empowered deliverer, and so that the later, messier judges can be measured against this clean pattern.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes Israel's sin, God's discipline, and God's rescue; it does not prescribe idolatry, nor does it command believers to seek military judges today. What is prescriptive by example is the crying out to God in distress and the confession that salvation belongs to the LORD. What endures is the pattern of grace: God disciplines, hears, and saves.

debates.question: In what sense did the LORD sell Israel and burn with anger against them?

views.label: Real divine judgment on covenant sin
views.case: The language of the LORD's anger and selling His people into an enemy's hand is the covenant curse of Deuteronomy 28 coming true; God actively, justly hands His people over to the consequences of their idolatry, and this is meant to be read as genuine judgment, not mere metaphor.

views.label: Fatherly discipline aimed at restoration
views.case: The very structure of the cycle, that servitude leads to a cry and the cry to deliverance, shows the anger is not the anger of abandonment but of a Father whose discipline is designed to bring His children back; the swiftness of the rescue reveals the mercy hidden inside the judgment.

debates.bearing: Both hold together: God's anger against sin is real and just, yet its aim is not destruction but the return and rest of His people, which is exactly how the New Testament reads God's discipline of those He loves, Hebrews 12:6.

debates.question: Why is Othniel given so little personal detail compared with later judges?

views.label: He is the deliberately clean template
views.case: The narrator strips away personal drama so that Othniel stands as the flawless pattern of the cycle; the sparseness is intentional, giving readers an uncomplicated standard before the flaws of Ehud, Gideon, and Samson begin to accumulate.

views.label: The focus is on God, not the man
views.case: The account keeps the spotlight on the LORD, who is the subject of nearly every decisive verb: He sells, He raises up, He gives the Spirit, He hands over the enemy; Othniel is thin as a character precisely so that the saving God fills the frame.

debates.bearing: Either way the reader is pointed away from the human hero toward the God who saves, guarding against hero-worship and preparing us to see that even the best judge is only a shadow of the true Deliverer.

study.originalLanguages: The vocabulary of this short passage carries its whole theology. Israel needs a moshia, a deliverer or saviour, and the LORD raises one up to yasha, to save, them. That verb yasha is the drumbeat of Judges and indeed of Scripture: a helpless people cannot save themselves, so God provides salvation from outside them; and the name Jesus, Yeshua, is built on this very root and means the LORD saves, Matthew 1:21. Othniel then becomes Israel's shophet, its judge, a word that in this book means far more than a courtroom official; the judge is a Spirit-raised rescuer and leader who defeats the oppressor and sets God's people right, and the word gives the book its name. The turning point is that the ruach, the Spirit of the LORD, comes upon him. The Hebrew ruach means spirit, breath, or wind, and its coming shows that the power to deliver is not native to Othniel but given by God. The words themselves preach the message: the LORD Himself, by His Spirit, saves His people through the deliverer He raises up.

typology.type: The Spirit-empowered deliverer raised up by God
typology.fulfillment: Christ, on whom the Spirit descends and rests without measure, the true and final Deliverer of His people, Isaiah 11:2 and Luke 4:18-21.

typology.type: The saviour who defeats the enemy and wins the people's freedom
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who conquers the greater enemies of sin, death, and the devil and sets His people free, Colossians 2:15 and Hebrews 2:14-15.

typology.type: The forty years of rest secured by the judge
typology.fulfillment: The lasting Sabbath rest that Christ gives, which never lapses and does not end with His death, Hebrews 4:8-10.

study.christ: Othniel is the first of the deliverers, and even in his bare account the outline of Christ is visible. The LORD raises up a saviour, sends His Spirit upon him, hands the enemy over, and grants the land rest. So the Father sent His Son, the Spirit descended and remained on Him, John 1:32-33, and He went out to war against the true oppressors of His people. Where Othniel defeated a Mesopotamian king, Jesus disarmed the powers of sin, death, and the devil at the cross, Colossians 2:15. Where Othniel's rest lasted forty years and then he died and the cycle resumed, Christ rose never to die again, and the rest He gives is a Sabbath rest that remains, Hebrews 4:9. Othniel is the shadow; Jesus, the LORD who saves, is the substance to whom every judge points.
study.god: The passage is finally about God, whose action fills nearly every decisive line. He is holy and just, and His anger burns against the idolatry of the people He redeemed; He will not treat covenant unfaithfulness lightly, and He hands His people over to servitude exactly as He warned. Yet He is rich in mercy: the moment they cry out, He moves to save. And He is the true author of the rescue from first to last. He raises up the deliverer, He pours out the Spirit, and He gives the enemy into Othniel's hand. Salvation is not achieved by Israel's strength or even mainly by Othniel's; it is God's gift of grace to a people who deserved only judgment, which is why the New Testament can say that salvation belongs to the LORD.

crossReferences.note: The covenant warning that unfaithfulness would leave Israel defeated before their enemies, fulfilled as the LORD sells them into Cushan-rishathaim's hand.
Othniel introduced earlier as a proven warrior who captured Kiriath-sepher and won Caleb's daughter Achsah, so he arrives already known as a faithful man.
I, even I, am the LORD, and apart from Me there is no Saviour; the raising up of a human moshia points to the God who alone truly saves.
You shall call His name Jesus, for He will save His people from their sins; the same root yasha that runs through Judges finds its fulfillment.
The rest under Joshua and the judges was not the final rest; a Sabbath rest remains for the people of God, given in Christ.

study.oneStory: Israel, only recently brought into the land God promised, forgets the LORD who saved them and is sold into slavery, until their cry rises and God raises up a Spirit-empowered deliverer who wins them rest. This first clean turn of the cycle sets the pattern for the whole book and for the whole of Scripture: a people who cannot save themselves and a God who keeps sending saviours. Each deliverance is a small rehearsal of the great one to come, when the LORD would send His own Son, Jesus, the true Deliverer on whom the Spirit rests, to win an everlasting rest for His people.

quiz.q: Why did the LORD sell Israel into the hand of Cushan-rishathaim?

options.text: Because Israel did evil, forgot the LORD, and served the Baals and the Asherahs.
options.note: Verses 7-8 tie the servitude directly to Israel's idolatry and forgetting of God.

options.text: Because Israel had grown militarily weak.
options.note: They were indeed overpowered, but the text names their sin, not weakness, as the reason.

options.text: Because God had simply abandoned them for good.
options.note: It was disciplinary anger aimed at restoration, not final abandonment; He soon saves them.

options.text: Because Cushan-rishathaim was stronger than the LORD.
options.note: The LORD Himself handed Israel over and later handed the king over; God is sovereign throughout.

quiz.q: What actually enabled Othniel to deliver Israel?

options.text: The Spirit of the LORD came upon him, and the LORD gave the enemy into his hand.
options.note: Verse 10 credits the Spirit and God's action, not Othniel's own strength.

options.text: His skill and courage as a proven warrior.
options.note: He was a proven warrior, Judges 1, but the decisive factor named is the Spirit of the LORD.

options.text: A large and well-equipped army.
options.note: The text says nothing of an army's size; it highlights the Spirit-empowered deliverer.

options.text: His own wisdom apart from God.
options.note: The passage traces the victory to the LORD, not to human wisdom apart from Him.

quiz.q: What does the title judge, shophet, mean in this book?

options.text: A Spirit-raised deliverer and leader who rescues God's people and sets them right.
options.note: In Judges the shophet is a saviour-leader, not merely a courtroom official.

options.text: A local ruler who governed and led the people.
options.note: There is a governing role, but the heart of it in this book is God-empowered deliverance.

options.text: A priest who offered sacrifices at the tabernacle.
options.note: That is the priestly office; the judge's role here is rescue and leadership, not sacrifice.

options.text: A judge who only settled legal disputes in court.
options.note: This narrows the word wrongly; the judges were raised-up rescuers, not mere magistrates.

quiz.q: Why is Othniel called the model judge?

options.text: His account shows the whole cycle cleanly, with no recorded flaws, as the standard the later judges fall short of.
options.note: The narrator presents the pattern in pure form before it decays in Ehud, Gideon, and Samson.

options.text: He was the strongest of all the judges.
options.note: He is a model, but not because of raw strength; it is the clean pattern of grace he displays.

options.text: He ruled for the longest time of any judge.
options.note: The forty years of rest belong to the land, and length of rule is not why he is the model.

options.text: He never needed God's help to win.
options.note: The opposite: he delivered only because the Spirit of the LORD came upon him.

quiz.q: How does Othniel's deliverance point forward to Christ?

options.text: A deliverer raised up by God and empowered by the Spirit defeats the enemy and wins rest, foreshadowing Jesus, the LORD who saves.
options.note: The Spirit-empowered saviour and the rest he wins are shadows fulfilled in Christ, Hebrews 4.

options.text: It teaches that God's people can save themselves if they try hard.
options.note: The passage teaches the opposite; salvation comes from God, which is the true link to Christ.

options.text: It shows that deliverance is only ever military and earthly.
options.note: The earthly rescue points beyond itself to Christ's rescue from sin, death, and the devil.

options.text: It has no connection to Christ at all.
options.note: The very name for save, yasha, is the root of the name Jesus; the link is direct.

questions: Israel's trouble began when they forgot the LORD their God. What helps you remember God and His goodness before drifting sets in, and where do you sense yourself forgetting Him now?
The moment Israel cried out, God moved to save. Is there a distress you have been carrying quietly that you need to bring to God in an honest cry for help?
The passage keeps the credit on God, who raised up the deliverer and gave the Spirit. Where are you tempted to trust your own strength or skill rather than the God who saves?
Othniel's rest lasted forty years and then the cycle resumed, but Christ's rest never ends. How does the promise of a rest that will not lapse steady you today?
Every judge is a shadow of Jesus, the true Deliverer. How does seeing Christ in this old story deepen your gratitude for the salvation He has already won?

translationNotes.note: Aram-naharaim means Aram of the two rivers; the BSB footnote identifies it as Mesopotamia, likely the region between the Euphrates and Balih Rivers in northwestern Mesopotamia.
The name Cushan-rishathaim may carry a deliberate note of mockery, since the second element can suggest doubled wickedness, fitting the book's habit of naming oppressors with a sneer.
Deliverer renders moshia, a saviour, from the same root yasha, to save, that runs through the whole book and stands behind the name Jesus, the LORD saves.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Judges 3:7-11. No em or en dashes present.
